jdbc_connector_workflow.overview:
This workflow guides you through creating a custom JDBC connector for any database using the IBM Watson Data Platform Connect SDK. You can choose between two approaches:
1. Codeless Mode: Quick setup using a pre-built Docker image (ghcr.io/thomasgloria/wdp-connect-sdk-gen-jdbc-connectors-forge:latest) - Best for standard JDBC connector use cases - No code generation or build process required - Faster deployment
2. Developer Mode: Full customizable connector code generation - Complete control over connector implementation - Includes code generation, customization, testing, and building - Best for custom implementations
Both modes end with deployment to AWS ECS Fargate.

prerequisites.requirement: Access to the target database for testing
JDBC driver information for the target database

phase.name: deployment_approach_selection
phase.title: Choose Deployment Approach
phase.description: Select between Codeless mode (using pre-built Docker image) or Developer mode (generating customizable connector code). This choice determines the workflow path.

step.action: Ask user to choose deployment approach
step.question: Choose your deployment approach for the JDBC connector:

suggestions.suggest: Codeless - Use pre-built Docker image (quick setup, standard use cases)
Developer - Generate customizable connector code (custom implementations)

step.action: Process user selection and set workflow path

actions.action: Inform user about pre-built Docker image usage
Create directory structure for DSL file
Skip code generation phases (2-9)
Proceed directly to Phase 10 (Flight App) with pre-built image
Inform user about custom code generation
Proceed to Phase 1 (Information Gathering)
Follow complete workflow through all phases

codeless_mode_details.overview: Codeless mode uses the pre-built Docker image: ghcr.io/thomasgloria/wdp-connect-sdk-gen-jdbc-connectors-forge:latest

benefits.benefit: Best for standard JDBC connector use cases

directory_structure.purpose: Store JDBC driver JAR for the connector

driver_handling.description: In Codeless mode, the JDBC driver JAR must be provided and saved to connectors-forge/codeless/jdbc/driver/driver.jar

automatic_download.condition: Driver is available from Maven Central or public repositories
automatic_download.action: Bob will automatically download the driver JAR
automatic_download.supported_databases: PostgreSQL, MySQL, MariaDB, H2, Derby, SQLite

manual_provision.condition: Driver is proprietary or requires license
manual_provision.action: Bob will prompt user for local driver JAR path
manual_provision.examples: Oracle, IBM Db2, Microsoft SQL Server (with license)

workflow_impact.skip: Phase 10: Flight App Generation (uses pre-built Docker image instead)
workflow_impact.execute: Phase 2: DSL Creation (includes driver JAR handling)

developer_mode_details.overview: Developer mode generates full customizable connector code from scratch

phase.name: information_gathering
phase.title: Gather Database Information
phase.description: Collect all necessary information about the target database before starting the connector creation process. For Codeless mode, also determine driver availability.

required_information.item: Connector label (e.g., MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle Database)
JDBC driver Maven coordinates (group:artifact:version) - for automatic download
Default port number for the database
Whether database supports schemas, catalogs, or both

step.action: Ask user for connector name and label
step.question: What is the name of the database you want to create a connector for?

suggestions.suggest: MySQL
PostgreSQL
Oracle
Microsoft SQL Server

step.action: Gather JDBC driver information
step.description: Collect JDBC driver details needed for DSL creation. This step applies to both Codeless and Developer modes, ensuring driver information is available before DSL generation.

information_to_collect.item: JDBC driver Maven coordinates (group:artifact:version) for dependency management

step.question: What is the JDBC driver class name for {database}?
step.note: This information is required for both Codeless and Developer modes

step.action: Gather JDBC driver Maven coordinates
step.question: What are the Maven coordinates for the JDBC driver (format: group:artifact:version)?
step.note: This will be used in the DSL file for dependency management

step.action: Handle driver JAR file for Codeless mode
step.description: For Codeless mode only, obtain the actual driver JAR file that will be bundled with the pre-built Docker image.
step.question: Do you have the JDBC driver JAR file available on your system, or would you like it to be automatically downloaded?

suggestions.suggest: I have the driver JAR at a local path
Automatically download the driver (for open-source databases)

step.note: This determines whether to copy from local path or download from Maven Central

step.action: Obtain driver JAR file based on user selection

path.name: local_driver
path.condition: User has driver at local path

substeps.substep: Ask user for the full path to the driver JAR file
Download driver using curl from Maven Central

step.action: Gather remaining database connection information

information_to_collect.item: Default port number for the database
Whether database supports schemas, catalogs, or both

step.note: Driver class name and Maven coordinates were already collected in Steps 2-3

phase.name: dsl_creation
phase.title: Create DSL Configuration
phase.description: Create a database-specific DSL configuration file based on DSL-jdbc.json template. This file defines all connector properties, driver information, and customizations. All driver information (class name, Maven coordinates, and JAR file for Codeless mode) has been collected in Phase 1, ensuring the DSL can be created with complete information.

step.action: Read the DSL-jdbc.json template file
Create new DSL file in connectors-forge/codeless/jdbc/ directory
Fill in connector metadata using information from Phase 1

fields.field: connector_description: Brief description of the connector

step.action: Configure JDBC driver information using data from Phase 1

fields.field: driver_class_name: Use the driver class name collected in Phase 1, Step 2
jdbc_url: Use the JDBC URL template collected in Phase 1, Step 6

step.note: All driver information was gathered before DSL creation to ensure completeness

step.action: Add driver dependencies using Maven coordinates from Phase 1
step.source: Use Maven coordinates collected in Phase 1, Step 3
step.note: For Codeless mode: This is for reference; actual driver JAR is in connectors-forge/codeless/jdbc/driver/
For Developer mode: This will be used by Gradle to download the driver during build

phase.name: deployment_preparation
phase.title: Prepare for Deployment (Codeless Mode Only)
phase.description: After DSL creation, prepare for deployment by selecting deployment type and creating the connector-config.env file with values from the DSL.

step.action: Ask user to select deployment type
step.question: Choose your deployment type for the JDBC connector:

suggestions.suggest: AWS - Deploy to AWS ECS Fargate
OpenShift - Deploy to Red Hat OpenShift
Self hosted - Deploy to your own server via SSH

step.action: Create connector-config.env from template
step.description: Copy connector-config.env.template to connector-config.env and populate it with values from the DSL file created in Phase 2.

substep.action: Read the DSL file to extract connector information
substep.note: Extract connector_name, connector_label, connector_description, and driver_class_name

mapping.source: Set based on deployment type: - AWS: /mnt/efs/driver.jar - OpenShift: /drivers/driver.jar

substep.success_message: ✅ connector-config.env created successfully with values from DSL

step.action: Confirm connector configuration with user
step.description: Display the connector-config.env contents to the user and ask for confirmation that the connector name (CONNECTOR_LABEL) that will be displayed in the UI is acceptable.

substep.action: Display contents and ask for confirmation
substep.question:
I've created the connector-config.env file with the following configuration:
[Display file contents here]
The connector will be displayed in the Cloud Pak for Data UI as: "{CONNECTOR_LABEL}"
Are you satisfied with this configuration, especially the connector name that will appear in the UI?

suggestions.suggest: Yes, the configuration looks good
No, I want to change the connector label (CONNECTOR_LABEL)
No, I want to modify other properties

path.name: change_label
path.condition: User wants to change connector label

actions.action: Ask user for new connector label
Update CONNECTOR_LABEL in connector-config.env
Update connector_label in DSL file for consistency
Show updated configuration and confirm again

path.name: modify_properties
path.condition: User wants to modify other properties

actions.action: Ask which property to modify
Update the specified property in connector-config.env
If driver-related, also update DSL file
Show updated configuration and confirm again

step.action: Create and review deploy.env for OpenShift deployment
step.description: For OpenShift deployment only, generate deploy.env from deploy.env.template with OpenShift-specific configuration, then ask user to review and update the values.
step.condition: Only execute if user selected OpenShift deployment in Step 1

substep.note: Copy template content as-is initially, user will update values for their OpenShift environment

substep.action: Display deploy.env and ask user to review
substep.question:
I've created the deploy.env file from the template for OpenShift deployment. This file contains OpenShift-specific deployment configuration.
[Display deploy.env contents here]
Please review the following key OpenShift settings: - OPENSHIFT_PROJECT: OpenShift project/namespace name - OPENSHIFT_REGISTRY: OpenShift container registry URL - IMAGE_NAME: Container image name - IMAGE_TAG: Container image tag - REPLICAS: Number of pod replicas - MEMORY_LIMIT: Memory limit for pods - CPU_LIMIT: CPU limit for pods
Do you need to update any values in deploy.env for your OpenShift environment?

suggestions.suggest: Yes, I need to update OpenShift configuration values
No, the default values are fine (for testing only)
Let me update the file manually and continue

actions.action: Ask which property to update
Ask for new value
Update the property in deploy.env
Show updated file and ask if more changes needed
Repeat until user is satisfied
Inform user: "The file is located at connectors-forge/codeless/jdbc/deploy.env"
Wait for user confirmation that updates are complete
Read and display updated file for verification

path.name: use_defaults
path.warning: Warn user if using defaults for production deployment

substep.success_message: ✅ deploy.env configured and ready for OpenShift deployment

step.action: Verify driver JAR is in place from Phase 1

validation.check: File size is greater than 0 bytes
Verify connector_label is user-friendly and descriptive
Ensure driver_class_name is correct for the database
Confirm jdbc_url template uses correct syntax
Validate driver_dependencies array is properly formatted
Confirm DSL file is saved in connectors-forge/codeless/jdbc/ directory

phase.name: label_confirmation
phase.title: Confirm Connector Label
phase.description: Ask the user to confirm the connector_label before proceeding, as this will be visible in the UI and should be user-friendly.

actions.action: Extract connector_label from the created DSL file
Use ask_followup_question to confirm with user
If user wants to change it, update the DSL file

phase.example_question: I've set the connector label to "{label}". This will be displayed in the Cloud Pak for Data UI. Is this label acceptable, or would you like to change it?

phase.name: code_generation
phase.title: Generate Connector Template Code
phase.description: Run the autoGenerateJavaJdbc Gradle task to generate the connector skeleton code from the DSL configuration.

step.action: Change to sdk-gen directory if not already there
Run the autoGenerateJavaJdbc task
step.note: The DSL file path is relative to sdk-gen directory

step.action: Wait for task completion and verify success

phase.name: dsl_relocation
phase.title: Move DSL to Connector Directory
phase.description: Move the DSL configuration file to the generated connector's resources directory for use in subsequent customization steps.

step.action: Identify the generated connector directory
Create the target directory if it doesn't exist
Copy DSL file to connector directory
step.tool: execute_command with cp or use read_file + write_to_file

phase.name: connector_customization
phase.title: Customize Connector Code
phase.description: Run the customizeJdbcConnector task to apply DSL configuration to the generated connector code, updating driver class names, connection URLs, and properties.

step.action: Run customizeJdbcConnector task
Verify customization success

what_it_does.update: Updates Connector.java with correct JDBC driver class name
Modifies getConnectionURL() method to build correct JDBC URL

phase.name: test_generation
phase.title: Generate Test Suite
phase.description: Generate comprehensive property validation tests for the connector using the generateJdbcTests task.

step.action: Run generateJdbcTests task
Verify test file generation

phase.name: code_formatting
phase.title: Apply Code Formatting
phase.description: Run spotlessApply to format all generated and customized code according to project standards.

step.action: Run spotlessApply task

phase.name: build
phase.title: Build Connector
phase.description: Build the connector without running tests to verify compilation and packaging.

step.action: Run build task excluding tests
Verify build success

phase.name: flight_app_generation
phase.title: Generate Flight App
phase.description:
Automatically generate a Flight app project that includes the newly created connector. This creates a deployable application with the connector integrated.
NOTE: This phase is ONLY executed in Developer mode. In Codeless mode, the pre-built Docker image (gloria2000/wdp-connect-sdk-gen-flight:latest) is used instead, so this phase is skipped.

step.action: Run autoGenerateFlightApp task
step.note: This task automatically determines the connector name from the DSL file

step.action: Verify flight app generation

success_indicators.indicator: Flight app directory created at subprojects/flight/
Connector dependency added to flight app build.gradle

step.action: Verify flight app can be built
step.note: This step is optional but recommended to verify the flight app is properly configured

what_it_does.action: Makes connector ready for Cloud Pak for Data integration

phase.name: deployment
phase.title: Deployment
phase.description: Deploy the connector to your chosen platform. This phase first asks the user to select their deployment type, then guides them through the appropriate deployment process based on their selection and mode (Codeless vs Developer).

step.action: Execute deployment based on selection from Phase 2.5
step.description: For Codeless mode, deployment type was already selected in Phase 2.5. Now execute the appropriate deployment script.

aws_deployment.condition: User selected AWS deployment in Phase 2.5

substep.action: Inform user about AWS deployment execution
substep.message:
Executing AWS deployment using the pre-built Docker image: ghcr.io/thomasgloria/wdp-connect-sdk-gen-jdbc-connectors-forge:latest
The deployment script will: 1. Pull the pre-built Docker image 2. Push it to your AWS ECR 3. Deploy to ECS Fargate with your connector configuration

substep.note: The script uses: - connector-config.properties for connector configuration - aws-deployment.properties for AWS settings - driver.jar from connectors-forge/codeless/lib/

openshift_deployment.condition: User selected OpenShift deployment in Phase 2.5

substep.action: Inform user about OpenShift deployment execution
substep.message:
Executing OpenShift deployment using the pre-built Docker image: ghcr.io/thomasgloria/wdp-connect-sdk-gen-jdbc-connectors-forge:latest
The deployment script will: 1. Pull the pre-built Docker image 2. Push it to your OpenShift registry 3. Deploy to OpenShift with your connector configuration

substep.note: The script uses: - connector-config.properties for connector configuration - openshift-deployment.properties for OpenShift settings - driver.jar from connectors-forge/codeless/lib/

self_hosted_deployment.condition: User selected Self hosted deployment in Phase 2.5

substep.action: Inform user about Self hosted deployment execution
substep.message:
Executing Self hosted deployment using the pre-built Docker image: ghcr.io/thomasgloria/wdp-connect-sdk-gen-jdbc-connectors-forge:latest
The deployment script will: 1. Connect to your remote server via SSH 2. Pull the pre-built Docker image on the remote server 3. Create and start a container with your connector configuration 4. Upload the JDBC driver and connector configuration files

substep.description: Guide user to create the deploy-container.env file from the template. This file contains SSH connection details and deployment configuration.

substep.action: Ask user for SSH connection details
substep.question:
To deploy to your self-hosted server, I need the following information:
1. SSH Host (remote server hostname or IP) 2. SSH User (username for SSH connection) 3. SSH Port (default: 22) 4. Port to expose the connector (e.g., 9443)
Would you like me to create the deploy-container.env file with these values?

suggestions.suggest: Yes, I'll provide the SSH connection details
No, I'll create the file manually

actions.action: Ask for SSH_HOST value
Ask for SSH_USER value
Ask for SSH_PORT value (default: 22)
Ask for PORT value (default: 9443)
Create deploy-container.env with provided values
Set IMAGE to ghcr.io/thomasgloria/wdp-connect-sdk-gen-jdbc-connectors-forge:latest
Set ENV_FILE to ./connector-config.env
Set DRIVER_DIR to ./driver
Set REPLACE to false
Provide instructions to copy template to deploy-container.env
List required fields to fill in
Wait for user confirmation that file is ready

mapping.source: User-provided remote server hostname or IP
Pre-built Docker image for codeless mode

validation.check: All required SSH properties are populated
IMAGE points to pre-built Docker image
ENV_FILE and DRIVER_DIR paths are correct

substep.success_message: ✅ deploy-container.env created successfully with SSH connection details

substep.action: Verify SSH connectivity with private key authentication
substep.description: Before deployment, verify that SSH connection to the remote server works using private key authentication.
substep.important_requirement:
⚠️ CRITICAL: You MUST be logged into your SSH server using private key authentication before running the deployment script.
The deployment script requires: - SSH private key authentication (password authentication is NOT supported) - Private key must be loaded in ssh-agent OR available at default locations (~/.ssh/id_rsa, ~/.ssh/id_ed25519, etc.) - No password prompts during SSH connection
If you haven't set up SSH key authentication yet, you must do so before proceeding.
substep.note: SSH authentication uses default SSH keys (~/.ssh/id_rsa, ~/.ssh/id_ed25519, etc.) or keys loaded in ssh-agent. Ensure SSH keys are properly configured.
substep.question:
⚠️ IMPORTANT: Before deploying, you MUST ensure you are logged into your SSH server using private key authentication.
Please verify: 1. You have SSH private key authentication configured (NOT password-based) 2. You can SSH to the remote server without password prompts: ssh -p {SSH_PORT} {SSH_USER}@{SSH_HOST} 3. Docker is installed and running on the remote server 4. Your SSH user has permissions to run Docker commands
Have you verified SSH private key authentication is working and Docker is available?

suggestions.suggest: Yes, I'm logged in with SSH private key authentication and Docker is ready
No, I need help setting up SSH private key authentication
No, I need to install Docker on the remote server

substep.note:
The script uses: - deploy-container.env for SSH connection and deployment settings - connector-config.env for connector configuration - driver/driver.jar for JDBC driver
The script will automatically load deploy-container.env on startup.

success_indicators.indicator: Docker image pulled on remote server
Deployment summary displayed with connection details

error.description: Cannot connect to remote server via SSH

troubleshooting.step: ⚠️ CRITICAL: Ensure you are logged into your SSH server using private key authentication (NOT password-based)
Verify SSH_HOST, SSH_USER, and SSH_PORT in deploy-container.env
Check SSH private key authentication is configured and working
Ensure your SSH private key is loaded in ssh-agent or available at default locations (~/.ssh/id_rsa, ~/.ssh/id_ed25519)
Verify you can connect without password prompts: ssh -p {SSH_PORT} {SSH_USER}@{SSH_HOST}
Ensure remote server is accessible from your network
If using ssh-agent, verify keys are loaded: ssh-add -l

error.description: Docker is not installed or not running on remote server

troubleshooting.step: SSH to remote server and check Docker: docker version
Start Docker service if not running
Ensure SSH user has Docker permissions (add to docker group)

error.description: The specified port is already in use by another container

troubleshooting.step: Check which container is using the port on remote server
Either stop the existing container or choose a different port
Set REPLACE=true in deploy-container.env to replace existing container
Or pass --replace flag when running the script

error.description: Failed to upload configuration files or driver to remote server

troubleshooting.step: Ensure remote server has sufficient disk space

substep.example_output:
========================================== Deployment Successful! ========================================== Container ID: abc123def456 Container Name: connector-20260622-062500 Remote Server: remote.example.com Port Mapping: 9443:9443
Connection URL: https://remote.example.com:9443
Next Steps: 1. Verify connector is accessible: curl https://remote.example.com:9443/health 2. Register with CP4D using the connection URL above ==========================================

substep.action: Ask if user wants to proceed to registration
substep.description: After successful self-hosted deployment, ask the user if they want to register the connector with Cloud Pak for Data.
substep.question:
Your JDBC connector has been successfully deployed to your self-hosted server.
Would you like to register it with Cloud Pak for Data now?
Registration will: - Make the connector available in your CP4D environment - Allow users to create database connections using this connector - Enable data discovery and access through CP4D

suggestions.suggest: Yes, register the connector with CP4D now
No, I'll register it later manually

step.action: Ask user to select deployment type
step.question: Choose your deployment type for the JDBC connector:

suggestions.suggest: Local Docker - Test on local machine
Serverless - Cloud deployment (placeholder)
Self-Hosted - Your own server
OpenShift - Enterprise platform

step.action: Process deployment type selection and execute appropriate deployment workflow

path.name: local_docker
path.title: Local Docker Deployment
path.description: Build and run connector locally using Docker for testing

substeps.substep: Run container with appropriate port mappings
Verify connector is accessible on localhost

path.name: serverless
path.title: Serverless Deployment
path.description: Deploy to cloud serverless platform (AWS ECS Fargate, etc.)

substeps.substep: Guide user to configure cloud provider settings

substep.action: Inform user about AWS ECS Fargate deployment
substep.message: The connector will be deployed to AWS ECS Fargate. You need to configure your AWS deployment settings in the aws-deployment.properties file.

substep.action: Guide user to configure AWS properties
substep.instructions: The deployment requires AWS configuration. You need to: 1. Copy aws-deployment.properties.template to aws-deployment.properties 2. Fill in your AWS details in aws-deployment.properties 3. Run the deployment script

substep.message:
Please fill in the following required fields in aws-deployment.properties: - AWS_ACCOUNT_ID: Your 12-digit AWS account ID - AWS_REGION: AWS region (e.g., us-east-1) - VPC_ID: Your VPC ID (e.g., vpc-xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx) - SUBNET_ID: Your subnet ID (e.g., subnet-xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx) - ECR_IMAGE_URI: ECR image URI (format: account-id.dkr.ecr.region.amazonaws.com/repository-name:tag) - ECS_CLUSTER: ECS cluster name (will be created if it doesn't exist)
Optional: You can also configure AWS credentials in the file, or use AWS CLI configured credentials.

substep.action: Wait for user confirmation that properties are configured
substep.question: Have you filled in all the required AWS configuration details in aws-deployment.properties?

suggestions.suggest: Yes, configuration is complete and ready to deploy
No, I need more time to configure

substep.action: Run the appropriate AWS deployment script based on mode

codeless_mode.note: The script will pull the pre-built Docker image (gloria2000/wdp-connect-sdk-gen-flight:latest), push it to ECR, and deploy to ECS Fargate with connector configuration from connector-config.env

developer_mode.note: The script will build the Docker image from your custom connector code, push it to ECR, and deploy to ECS Fargate

substep.action: Ask if user wants to proceed to registration
substep.description: After successful AWS deployment, ask the user if they want to register the connector with Cloud Pak for Data.
substep.question:
Your JDBC connector has been successfully deployed to AWS ECS Fargate.
Would you like to register it with Cloud Pak for Data now?
Registration will: - Make the connector available in your CP4D environment - Allow users to create database connections using this connector - Enable data discovery and access through CP4D

suggestions.suggest: Yes, register the connector with CP4D now
No, I'll register it later manually

prerequisites.requirement: Cloud provider CLI installed and configured
Valid cloud provider credentials with appropriate permissions
Network configuration (VPC, subnet, etc.) set up

path.name: self_hosted
path.title: Self-Hosted Deployment
path.description: Deploy to your own server infrastructure

substeps.substep: Push image to your private registry
Provide deployment instructions for your server

path.name: openshift
path.title: OpenShift Deployment
path.description: Deploy to Red Hat OpenShift platform

substeps.substep: Verify deployment and provide access details

substep.message: The connector will be deployed to Red Hat OpenShift. You need to configure your OpenShift deployment settings in the openshift-deployment.properties file.

substep.action: Guide user to configure OpenShift properties
substep.instructions: The deployment requires OpenShift configuration. You need to: 1. Ensure you have access to an OpenShift cluster 2. Configure openshift-deployment.properties with your cluster details 3. Run the deployment script

substep.message:
Please fill in the following required fields in openshift-deployment.properties: - OPENSHIFT_SERVER: OpenShift API server URL - OPENSHIFT_TOKEN: Authentication token for OpenShift - OPENSHIFT_PROJECT: Project/namespace name - IMAGE_REGISTRY: Container image registry URL - APP_NAME: Application name for the connector
Optional: Additional configuration for routes, resource limits, etc.

substep.action: Wait for user confirmation that properties are configured
substep.question: Have you filled in all the required OpenShift configuration details in openshift-deployment.properties?

suggestions.suggest: Yes, configuration is complete and ready to deploy
No, I need more time to configure

substep.note: The script will build the container image, push it to the registry, and deploy to OpenShift

substep.action: Ask if user wants to proceed to registration
substep.description: After successful OpenShift deployment, ask the user if they want to register the connector with Cloud Pak for Data.
substep.question:
Your JDBC connector has been successfully deployed to OpenShift.
Would you like to register it with Cloud Pak for Data now?
Registration will: - Make the connector available in your CP4D environment - Allow users to create database connections using this connector - Enable data discovery and access through CP4D

suggestions.suggest: Yes, register the connector with CP4D now
No, I'll register it later manually

prerequisites.requirement: Docker or Podman installed and running
Valid OpenShift cluster access with appropriate permissions

phase.name: cp4d_registration
phase.title: Cloud Pak for Data Registration (Optional)
phase.description: After successful deployment, optionally register the JDBC connector with Cloud Pak for Data. This makes the connector available for use in the CP4D environment.

step.action: Ask if user wants to register connector with CP4D
step.description: After deployment completes successfully, ask the user if they want to proceed with registering the connector in Cloud Pak for Data.
step.question:
Your JDBC connector has been successfully deployed.
Would you like to register it with Cloud Pak for Data now?
Registration will: - Make the connector available in your CP4D environment - Allow users to create database connections using this connector - Enable data discovery and access through CP4D

suggestions.suggest: Yes, register the connector with CP4D now
No, I'll register it later manually
Help - I need more information about registration

step.action: Determine registration approach based on deployment mode

path.name: developer_mode
path.condition: Developer mode was selected in Phase 0

substep.description: Before registration, you need to create a properties file with your CP4D environment configuration. This file is not visible to me (it's in .bobignore), so you need to create it manually.
substep.instructions:
Create a properties file in: sdk-gen/subprojects/flight/src/dist/resources/payload/envs/
Use a descriptive name like: myenv.properties, production.properties, or dev.properties
Base your file on the template at: sdk-gen/subprojects/flight/src/dist/resources/payload/envs/template.properties
Required properties: - apikey: Your IBM Cloud API key - auth_uri: Authentication endpoint (default: https://iam.cloud.ibm.com/identity/token) - datasource_types_uri: Datasource types API endpoint - flight_uri: URI of your deployed Flight service (grpc://host:port) - ssl_certificate_validation: Set to true/false
substep.confirmation_question: Have you created the properties file with your CP4D environment configuration? Please provide the name of your properties file (without .properties extension).

substep.question: Would you like me to execute the registration, or would you prefer to do it yourself?

suggestions.suggest: Automatic - Execute registration for me
Manual - I'll do it myself with instructions

substep.note: Replace {env_name} with the properties file name provided by user
substep.success_message:
✅ Registration complete!
Your JDBC connector has been registered with Cloud Pak for Data. The connector is now available for use in your CP4D environment.

substep.instructions:
To register your JDBC connector with Cloud Pak for Data:
1. Ensure you have created your properties file in: sdk-gen/subprojects/flight/src/dist/resources/payload/envs/{your_env_name}.properties
2. Navigate to the sdk-gen directory: cd sdk-gen
3. Run the registration task with your environment name: ./gradlew register -Penv={your_env_name}
Replace {your_env_name} with your properties file name (without .properties extension) For example, if your file is myenv.properties, use: -Penv=myenv
The registration task will: - Authenticate with IBM Cloud using your API key - Register the connector datasource type with CP4D - Configure the Flight service endpoint - Make the connector available in your CP4D environment
Let me know once you've completed the registration or if you encounter any issues.

path.name: codeless_mode
path.condition: Codeless mode was selected in Phase 0

substep.description: Before registration, you need to create a properties file based on the provided template. This file is not visible to me (it's in .bobignore), so you need to create it manually.

openshift_deployment_note.important:
If you deployed to OpenShift (Phase 11), the deploy-openshift.sh script displayed the Flight hostname in the deployment details output. You'll need this for the flight_hostname configuration below.
Look for the "Flight Hostname" in the deployment output from deploy-openshift.sh. Use this hostname value (without grpc:// or grpc+tls:// prefix) for flight_hostname.

substep.instructions:
1. Review the template file at: connectors-forge/scripts/register-envs.properties.template
2. Copy the template to create your configuration: cp connectors-forge/scripts/register-envs.properties.template connectors-forge/scripts/register-envs.properties
3. Edit register-envs.properties and configure:
AUTHENTICATION (choose ONE): - Option 1: IBM Cloud API Key apikey=your_ibm_cloud_api_key_here - Option 2: Username/Password (on-premises) username=your_username password=your_password
FLIGHT SERVICE (required): - flight_hostname=your-flight-hostname (just the hostname, no grpc:// or grpc+tls:// prefix) - flight_port=your-flight-port
NOTE FOR OPENSHIFT DEPLOYMENT: If you deployed to OpenShift, use the Flight hostname from the deployment output. The deploy-openshift.sh script displayed this in the deployment details.
ENDPOINTS: - auth_uri=https://iam.cloud.ibm.com/identity/token (or your CP4D auth endpoint) - datasource_types_uri=https://api.dataplatform.cloud.ibm.com:443/v2/datasource_types (or your CP4D endpoint)
substep.confirmation_question: Have you created register-envs.properties from the template and configured all required values?

substep.question: Would you like me to execute the registration, or would you prefer to do it yourself?

suggestions.suggest: Automatic - Execute registration for me
Manual - I'll do it myself with instructions

substep.description: Runs the OS-specific registration script that handles authentication, connector registration, and Flight service configuration.
substep.success_message:
✅ Registration complete!
Your JDBC connector has been registered with Cloud Pak for Data. The connector is now available for use in your CP4D environment.

substep.instructions:
To register your JDBC connector with Cloud Pak for Data:
1. Ensure you have configured the properties file at: connectors-forge/scripts/register-envs.properties
For Unix/Linux/macOS: 2. Ensure the script has execute permissions: chmod +x connectors-forge/scripts/register-connector.sh
3. Run the registration script: connectors-forge/scripts/register-connector.sh
For Windows: 2. Run the PowerShell registration script: .\connectors-forge\scripts\register-connector.ps1
The registration script will: - Read your configuration from register-envs.properties - Authenticate with IBM Cloud or CP4D - Register the connector datasource type - Configure the Flight service endpoint - Make the connector available in your CP4D environment
Let me know once you've completed the registration or if you encounter any issues.

step.action: Provide registration information
step.information:
Registration Process Overview:
Registration makes your JDBC connector available in Cloud Pak for Data by: 1. Registering the connector as a datasource type in CP4D 2. Configuring the connection to your deployed Flight service 3. Making the connector discoverable to CP4D users
After registration, users can: - Create database connections using your connector - Browse database schemas and tables - Query data through CP4D - Use the connector in data integration workflows
Requirements: - Successfully deployed Flight service (from Phase 11) - CP4D environment access (IBM Cloud or on-premises) - Valid credentials (API key or username/password) - Network connectivity between CP4D and your Flight service
You can register now or later. The registration scripts can be run manually at any time.

completion_criteria.criterion: DSL configuration file created and validated

criterion_conditional.criterion: DSL moved to connector resources directory
Flight app generated with connector integrated
Directory structure created for DSL file and driver JAR
Driver JAR obtained (downloaded or copied from local)

developer_mode.step: Review generated connector code in subprojects/jdbc_{database}/
Review generated flight app in subprojects/flight/

common_steps.step: Configure connector in Cloud Pak for Data using the gRPC endpoint

error.action: Check Gradle output for specific error messages
Verify DSL file syntax is valid JSON
Ensure all required fields are filled in DSL
Check that working directory is correct (sdk-gen/)
Consult database documentation for JDBC driver details
Check Maven Central for driver coordinates

error.description: Driver JAR could not be downloaded from Maven Central (Codeless mode)

actions.action: Verify driver coordinates are correct (group:artifact:version)
Check internet connectivity
Verify Maven Central is accessible (https://repo1.maven.org/maven2/)
Try alternative download method (curl/wget directly)
Check if driver requires authentication or license acceptance
If driver is proprietary, request local path from user

error.fallback: Ask user to provide driver JAR from local system

error.description: Driver JAR could not be copied to target location (Codeless mode)

actions.action: Verify source file path exists and is accessible
Check file is a valid JAR file (not corrupted)
Verify target directory exists: sdk-gen/subprojects/connectors_forge_jdbc/src/main/resources/
Create target directory if missing: mkdir -p sdk-gen/subprojects/connectors_forge_jdbc/src/main/resources/
Check file permissions for read/write access
Verify sufficient disk space available
Try copying with explicit permissions: cp -f source destination

error.description: Driver JAR not found at expected location for Codeless mode deployment

actions.action: Verify driver.jar exists at: sdk-gen/subprojects/connectors_forge_jdbc/src/main/resources/driver.jar
Check if driver download/copy step completed successfully
Review previous step outputs for errors
Re-run driver provisioning step (Phase 2, Step 6)
Verify file was not accidentally deleted or moved
Check file permissions and ownership

error.description: Provided driver JAR is invalid, corrupted, or incompatible (Codeless mode)

actions.action: Verify file is a valid JAR file (check file extension is .jar)
Check file size is greater than 0 bytes
Try extracting JAR contents to verify integrity: jar -tf driver.jar
Verify JAR contains expected JDBC driver class
Check if JAR is compatible with Java version being used
Request user to provide a different driver JAR from official source
Download driver from official vendor website if available

error.action: Check for compilation errors in generated code
Verify JDBC driver dependency is correct

actions.action: Verify deployment platform CLI/tools are installed and configured
Check credentials and permissions for the selected platform
Ensure network configuration is correct (VPC, subnet, firewall rules, etc.)
Verify Docker is running (if applicable)
Review deployment script/command output for specific errors
Check image registry access and URI format
Verify resource quotas and limits on the platform
Consult platform-specific documentation for troubleshooting
